text: the pay is insufficient even for faculty who have been here many years.  students, who we encourage to get an education, see people with 10 + years of university and phds earning less than a manager at a starbucks with a high school diploma.  they take note of this and basically think we are stupid to go into a profession that pays so little.  the college's view is that if anyone in teaching wants to earn good money, get out of teaching as fast as possible, and go into administration because that's where the money is.  but, what if teaching is our gift, and we want to share that, but also earn a decent salary--the college doesn't care, the board and the legislature doesn't have a clue, and they think administrators are more qualified (nothing could be further from the truth) or that they have more responsibility . . . but they are constantly saying that we, the faculty, are face-to-face with students and that's the most important thing, but obviously it isn't because they won't pay us what we're worth.  so many people are leaving this profession because of poor treatment.  never say "people don't go into education for the money, it's a vocation" no it isn't, it's a job, a job that requires years of graduate school.  we earn and deserve decent pay--we're not mother theresa, we're people who want to look after our families.

text:
i having been a student in the bad old days some of the issues with food was that i did not qualify for any assistance,  my undergraduate was from texas a&m and they had a great program from former students that supplied loans that did not have interest to pay tuition which could be paid off at the end of semester.  there were enough jobs around that i could work several and pay it off.  graduated without debt.  here there are not enough jobs even though the city is much bigger and federal loans is the only way, especially for graduate students, to make ends meet.  the grad students at texas had health insurance which eased my burden.  of course, the state had approved such a thing.  

one of my biggest peeves was from last years ice storm.  students were stranded on campus and unm did not do anything.  calling school closed late and no preparations was very dangerous.  i was busing students to my daughters trailer since she lives on south campus.  city buses were stranded and i only found out later that the sun van has 4-wheel drive.  still there were students that were stuck at the library and had no way home and the library was closing.  i would love to see a plan if students are trapped where they can shelter until the danger passes and they can get off campus.

text:
i teach one class at a charter high school in the south valley. i take food there regularly because they don't "like" the food the school serves and always complain they are hungry, so they gladly take my packaged food which is primarily junk food. but i figure a treat is a treat, and if they know someone cares, that could mean a lot to them. the systems aren't working for everyone. i'm so lucky to be in such a place of privilege that i didn't earn. i lead a very privileged life compared to the students at the charter school. for example:
there was a shooting a few weeks ago. two students were killed. another student misses her dad who left her a decade ago to go to mexico once he was "wanted" and has come back to the area but hasn't responded that he wants to see her. another student lost a 2-year-old cousin about 2 weeks ago. most all live in single parent homes. apartments. south valley houses with extended families sometimes. these kids have a very hard life, right here in albuquerque where we ought to be able to do better. 
i share this because i can see the disparity and it breaks my heart. 
thanks for initiating this survey to see what needs are out there.
